*Case study generated by Claude from transcript of recording*

Naina Rao, a promising young journalist, found herself facing a new and daunting challenge when she was hired as the news director for a public radio station in Guam. Despite her passion for the work and excitement about the opportunity, Naina was quickly overwhelmed by the realities of her new leadership role.

In a coaching session with veteran media executive Alan Soon, Naina opened up about the unique obstacles she was confronting. As a US territory in the Pacific, Guam had a vastly different media landscape than the mainland, with a local audience more interested in news from neighboring Asian countries than the coverage provided by the station's NPR affiliation.

Adding to the complexity, Naina's manager was frequently absent, leaving her to essentially "manage her manager" and navigate tricky budgeting decisions without clear guidance. "It's a classic situation where I have to manage my manager," Naina lamented. "He's never done this before. And he doesn't like spending money."

Despite these challenges, Naina expressed a genuine passion for the work, particularly the opportunity to deeply engage with the local community through interviews. "I genuinely love getting to know people and that's why I love interviewing people," she shared.

However, the stress and overwhelm were taking a toll. Naina admitted that "most of the time it brings me stress. I'm overwhelmed. It's a lot." She even started to question whether her long-term goal of becoming an international correspondent was truly the right path.

In the coaching session, Alan empathized with Naina's experience, acknowledging the common, but often unspoken, difficulties that come with transitioning from reporting to management. "The first few months in that process was really quite scary because there was the expectation that I would understand all the things that I would do," he shared, drawing on his own past experiences.

Rather than focus on the problems, Alan encouraged Naina to reflect on what she hoped to gain from this role, and how she could leverage her unique position in Guam to build expertise and a personal brand as a foreign journalist. He also stressed the importance of building a network of mentors who could provide guidance and support as she navigated the new challenges of leadership.

"You don't have to do this alone," Alan reassured Naina. "You can say to someone, 'Hey, I'm trying to hire. I have three great candidates. I don't know how to make a choice between them. Can you help me think this through?' And that's totally okay."

By the end of the session, Naina felt empowered to reframe her perspective, shifting from "What do I do?" to "How do I think about this?" This subtle but important distinction, Alan explained, would allow Naina to tap into her existing skills and instincts, rather than feeling paralyzed by the unknowns of management.

Ultimately, Alan saw Naina as being well-positioned for success, with the right mindset and capabilities to thrive in her new role. By embracing the challenges as learning opportunities and building a support system of trusted advisors, Naina could navigate the transition to news director with confidence and turn her unique situation in Guam into a powerful advantage.
